title: meaning: Resurgence of Pandiyan History
description: meaning: Resurgence of Pandiyan History is a book by K. Senthil Mallar. The book allegedly claims the Dalit community called Pallar, were among the rulers of the Pandya kingdom.
descriptionParagraphs: meaning: Resurgence of Pandiyan History is a book by K. Senthil Mallar. The book allegedly claims the Dalit community called Pallar, were among the rulers of the Pandya kingdom.

What makes it interesting is the way a book becomes legible to officials as a political instrument rather than a neutral cultural object. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in India. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The Tamil Nadu government banned this Tamil book on 30 May 2013 on grounds that it may cause violence and promote discord among communities. The book allegedly claims the Dalit community called Pallar, were among the. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: meaning: Resurgence of Pandiyan History is a book by K. Senthil Mallar. The book allegedly claims the Dalit community called Pallar, were among the rulers of the Pandya kingdom.
banningParagraphs: meaning: Resurgence of Pandiyan History entered censorship debates as a book associated with politics, public argument, and state power. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around political control and political dissent.

The earliest event currently captured here is 2013 in India, where Government of Tamil Nadu banned publication, sale, or possession. The Tamil Nadu government banned this Tamil book on 30 May 2013 on grounds that it may cause violence and promote discord among communities. The book allegedly claims the Dalit community called Pallar, were among the. counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
